title: Loops: Running One Check Against Every Reading
learningObjectives: Iterate over a list with a for loop instead of manually indexing element by element
Use enumerate() to access both index and value during iteration
Write a while loop for a condition-driven repeat, e.g. 'keep charging until full'
Recognize the conditions that produce an infinite loop before ever running one
whyItMatters: Classifying one demand reading (Control Flow) doesn't scale to 24 readings a day, let alone the 8,760 in a year. Loops are what let a single threshold check run once and apply to every reading in the Digital Twin without copy-pasting code per reading.
capstoneContribution: The batch-classification pass over the Digital Twin's rolling reading window — turns the single-reading threshold check into something that runs across a full day, then a full year, of data.

problem.summary: 24 readings, one working single-value classifier, and no way to apply it to all of them.
problem.content: You have a list of 24 hourly demand readings and one working threshold classifier (from Control Flow) that only handles a single value at a time.

motivation.summary: Real energy datasets are thousands of readings, not dozens.
motivation.content: Every later mission processes not one data point but thousands — a full year of hourly demand is 8,760 readings. A for loop is what makes 'run this once' become 'run this for every reading' without rewriting the logic 8,760 times.

theory.summary: for iterates a fixed sequence; while repeats until a condition becomes false.
theory.content: `for reading in readings_kw:` binds `reading` to each element in turn and executes the loop body once per element. `enumerate(readings_kw)` yields `(index, value)` pairs when you need position and value together. `while condition:` repeats as long as `condition` stays true — useful when you don't know the number of iterations in advance (e.g. 'keep discharging the battery until it's empty'), but risks an infinite loop if the condition never becomes false.

visualExplanation.summary: An animated pointer sweeps across the row-of-boxes list, running the classifier on each box.
visualExplanation.content: An animated pointer moves across the row-of-boxes list visual from the Lists lesson, pausing on each box while the classification logic runs against it and printing a result per box before moving on.

interactiveDemo.summary: Step through a for loop one iteration at a time.
interactiveDemo.content: Step through a for loop one iteration at a time via a 'step' button, watching the loop variable's value update and the classification output accumulate below.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: classify every reading in a list with a for loop, then with enumerate().

independentCoding.summary: Loop over battery readings with enumerate(), printing only the low ones.
independentCoding.content: Given a list of battery state-of-charge percentages recorded every hour, write a for loop with `enumerate()` that prints `Hour {i}: {value}% - LOW` for any reading below 25, and nothing for the rest.

debugging.summary: Fix a while loop whose condition variable is never updated.
debugging.content:
```python
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print("charging...")
```
This never terminates because `count` is never changed inside the loop. Identify the missing line and fix it.

reflection.summary: Infinite loops are a predictable mistake, not a mysterious bug.
reflection.content: Why is a while loop with a condition that never changes exactly as dangerous as it sounds, and how would you notice it before running the code rather than after?

teachBack.summary: Explain for-over-a-list vs. while-with-a-counter to someone who thinks they're interchangeable.
teachBack.content: Explain the difference between a for loop over a list and a while loop with a counter, to someone who thinks they're always interchangeable.

miniProject.summary: Loop over the full rolling window instead of just the latest reading.
miniProject.content: Extend the sensor-intake mini project: loop over the full rolling window of 6 readings and print a per-hour classification line for each, instead of just the single latest reading.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This becomes the Digital Twin's first per-timestep processing pass.
portfolioEvidence.content: The batch classification loop becomes the first version of the Digital Twin's per-timestep processing pass, later replaced by a vectorized pandas operation in Mission 2.

labs.title: Batch Classifier
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise requiring the learner to loop over a list of readings and produce correct per-reading output, including a deliberately included while-loop variant that must terminate correctly.

simulations.title: Loop Step Visualizer
simulations.description: Step-through animation of a for/while loop executing against a list, highlighting the current element and accumulated output.

flashcards.front: What two things does enumerate(readings_kw) give you on each iteration that a plain for loop doesn't?
flashcards.back: The index (position) of the current element alongside the value itself, as an (index, value) pair.

flashcards.front: What's the single most common cause of an infinite while loop?
flashcards.back: The condition being checked never changes inside the loop body, so it stays true forever — usually because the variable that should be updated (e.g. a counter) was forgotten.

rubric: Correctly identifies why a given while loop never terminates and explains the fix, not just applies it blindly
for-loop solution uses enumerate() only where position is actually needed, not reflexively everywhere
Batch classifier produces correct output for every element in the list, including boundary-value readings

references.title: Python Language Reference — The for statement
Python Language Reference — The while statement
